channel.title: Combined Economist RSS Feed
channel.description: Combined feed of multiple Economist RSS sources with archive.is/o/nuunc links

item.title: The War Room newsletter: How the Iran war is escalating
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, explains that the conflict is intensifying in four ways

item.title: The view from Tehrangeles
item.description: The Iranian diaspora cheered Khamenei’s death. They are divided over what should come next

item.title: The Iran energy shock reverberates across financial markets
item.description: For investors, the war is like the invasion of Ukraine all over again

item.title: Should the Gulf states join attacks on Iran?
item.description: A lack of trust in America and Israel, internal divisions and the risk of domestic strife all hamper a unified response

item.title: China’s AI giants are handing out cash to lure in users
item.description: How long can their ferocious rivalry last?

item.title: There are 56 ethnicities in China—and 55 are getting squashed
item.description: China wants its minorities decorative, but not distinct

item.title: Iran’s defiant regime picks a new supreme leader
item.description: The appointment suggests the Revolutionary Guards have the upper hand

item.title: Germany’s Greens have come back to win in Baden-Württemberg
item.description: The defeat of the Christian Democrats is a poor sign for Friedrich Merz

item.title: What a second week of war will bring
item.description: The conflict is turning into a test of will between Iran and its adversaries

item.title: The Iran war puts Asia in an energy panic
item.description: Stranded Gulf supplies are choking off the region’s economies

item.title: Why MAGA backs Donald Trump’s war—for now
item.description: If the conflict in Iran drags on, will his America First base stick with him?

item.title: Would America be in recession without the super-rich?
item.description: American anxieties are K-shaped. The economy is more like a backslash

item.title: Is India the fourth- or fifth-biggest economy? It does not matter
item.description: Politicians boasting about India’s economic prowess are laying a trap for themselves

item.title: The Iran war puts Vladimir Putin in a tough spot
item.description: Higher oil prices are poor compensation for showing Russia’s weakness

item.title: Checks and Balance: Pete Hegseth and the risks of a macho military
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our New York bureau chief, on why divisive machismo is at the core of the Trump administration’s military policy

item.title: The Iran war has entered a new phase
item.description: Both sides have changed their tactics, our data analysis finds

item.title: Israel sees a chance to destroy Hizbullah at last
item.description: Iran’s most powerful proxy, though weakened, is still in the fight

item.title: Can Ukraine help defeat Iran’s drone swarms?
item.description: Thousands of Shaheds are raining down on the Gulf

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: A war without a strategy
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s cover

item.title: What is your maximum heart rate?
item.description: We tell you how to find out

item.title: Anthropic’s boss apologises for bashing Pentagon—but still plans to sue
item.description: The AI lab is the first American firm to be labelled a supply-chain risk

item.title: Kristi Noem’s ignoble legacy as homeland security secretary
item.description: She will not be remembered fondly by Americans or her workforce

item.title: Faecal transplants—a treatment for bipolar disorder?
item.description: Initial results from a test are intriguing

item.title: Israel and America want the Kurds to join the fight in Iran
item.description: Kurdish militias are weighing a risky bid to redraw their neighbour’s borders

item.title: An AI disaster is getting ever closer
item.description: The spat between America’s government and Anthropic intensifies an alarming trend

item.title: Nigel Farage and Zack Polanski: best of frenemies
item.description: Britain’s twin populists have a symbiotic relationship

item.title: AI danger gets real
item.description: The squabble between America’s government and Anthropic makes an AI disaster more likely

item.title: Allegations against a corruption watchdog rock Malaysian politics
item.description: The prime minister, Anwar Ibrahim, repeats his predecessors’ answer

item.title: Welcome to Kashiwazaki, home to the world’s largest nuclear plant
item.description: What one small city says about modern Japan’s development

item.title: China sets its lowest growth target for a generation
item.description: In macroeconomics, modesty is not always a virtue

item.title: China’s first railway project in the EU is open at last
item.description: Once a show of largesse, it now reflects China’s struggles on the continent

item.title: States are embracing the MAHA food agenda
item.description: Robert F. Kennedy junior and company have built a powerful coalition

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
How the Danes and Swedes handle populism
item.description: Denmark’s Mette Frederiksen calls an election to take on the hard right again

item.title: Meet the weekend warriors preparing to defend Europe from Russia
item.description: Lithuania’s citizen-soldiers are part of growing volunteer forces in the Baltics

item.title: Feted by Europe’s left, Spain’s Pedro Sánchez is unloved at home
item.description: The prime minister is trying to become an icon of resistance to Donald Trump

item.title: Why one of Germany’s richest regions is gripped with anxiety
item.description: Baden-Württemberg’s election is overshadowed by worries over deindustrialisation

item.title: Formula One is attracting a different sort of fan
item.description: Lifestyle as much as motor racing is drawing in newcomers

item.title: A short guide to email opening lines
item.description: “I hope you are well” and other classics

item.title: Bayer spies an end to a long legal battle
item.description: After a settlement over Roundup, what about break-up?

item.title: In African development, big is beautiful again
item.description: With aid declining, grand visions for growth are back in fashion

item.title: Thousands of Africans are fighting for Russia in Ukraine
item.description: African governments are belatedly trying to stop them

item.title: Javier Milei aggressively celebrates a string of successes
item.description: Argentina’s president has much to shout about. But job worries loom

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu has his war
item.description: If it goes wrong, he will also get the blame

item.title: Investigative journalism in India is under threat
item.description: Politicians and tycoons are tightening their grip

item.title: Russia’s Starlink shutdown is a blow to its soldiers and drones
item.description: Launching its own satellite network is not realistic

item.title: Donald Trump must stop soon
item.description: His ill-considered conflict risks descending into chaos

item.title: How the latest regional conflict is reshaping the Middle East
item.description: Neither Iran, nor Israel, nor the Gulf will ever be quite the same

item.title: To understand why countries grow, look at their firms
item.description: The third way in development economics

item.title: It’s time to unleash Europe’s pensions
item.description: One reform offers both security in old age and dynamism now

item.title: India’s economy is not as big as economists thought
item.description: But it is growing faster

item.title: Americans’ electricity bills are up. Don’t blame AI
item.description: Were it not for data centres, prices might be even higher

item.title: European pensions are a $30trn missed opportunity
item.description: If only more countries went Dutch

item.title: In times of chaos, Europe is the muddled power the world needs
item.description: In praise of the dreaded European working group

item.title: A once-proud tradition is becoming awkward for elite universities
item.description: The slow demise of the swim test

item.title: What the heirs to General Electric did next
item.description: Their success illustrates the benefits of breaking up—and more

item.title: The Iran war has been a stunning aerial success
item.description: Even if, at the political level, its direction is a mess

item.title: A basket of new fruit varieties is coming your way
item.description: Thank gene editing

item.title: China needs a more ambitious growth target
item.description: Otherwise a fourth year of deflation awaits

item.title: The start of the Iran war was determined by spying success
item.description: But even when assassinations come off, the long-term consequences are unclear

item.title: The Iran war in maps and charts
item.description: Visualisations that tell the story of the conflict

item.title: The New President of the United States
item.description: Our report from 1901

item.title: How 250 years of immigration shaped America
item.description: Cycles of openness and restriction are as old as the country itself

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is the big winner from the Iran war, for now
item.description: To avoid a row with Donald Trump, Western allies are endorsing Israeli theories of war

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is the big winner from the Iran war, for now
item.description: To avoid a row with Donald Trump, Western allies are endorsing Israeli theories of war

item.title: Donald Trump bets big on the Iran war
item.description: If it fails to pay off politically, will he cut his losses or double down?

item.title: Why war isn’t always good for defence stocks
item.description: They win only if governments want just enough weapons—but not too many

item.title: The nightmare war scenario is becoming reality in energy markets
item.description: The longer the war in the Gulf, the harsher the global economic fallout

item.title: Are Gulf states running out of missile interceptors?
item.description: Their rulers insist not but they are burning through their stockpiles fast

item.title: The Iran war is a jolt to Dubai’s business model
item.description: Can the emirate keep people and investors coming?

item.title: What France’s new nuclear-arms doctrine means for Europe
item.description: Emmanuel Macron will build more warheads and include allies

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The new cancel culture on campuses
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, reports on the pressures facing American universities in the Trump era

item.title: Cuba’s economic divides are widening
item.description: Luck and guile increasingly determine who gets how much

item.title: Why Ali Khamenei may have welcomed the nature of his death
item.description: The ideology of martyrdom could prolong Iran’s regime—and the war

item.title: The Iran war is rapidly engulfing the region
item.description: How far could each side escalate?

item.title: Hizbullah, the reluctant proxy, strikes at Israel
item.description: But the group’s limited actions suggest that it does not want to be in a war that could finish it off

item.title: Can Viktor Orban be beaten?
item.description: We are tracking the polls for Hungary’s election in April

item.title: Data centres in space: less crazy than you think
item.description: They could be cheaper than ones on Earth, with the right technology

item.title: Airlines take a hit from hostilities in the Middle East
item.description: The war will hurt the Gulf carriers—and the whole industry too

item.title: The War Room newsletter: A widening war in the Middle East
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, asks how long the escalation in Iran can last

item.title: The modest start of America’s foreign forays
item.description: Commemorating the first amphibious assault

item.title: Fifteen years after Fukushima, Japan faces an energy dilemma
item.description: Restarting the world’s largest nuclear plant will not resolve its problems

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The daunting quest for critical minerals
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s covers

item.title: China’s ice-cold calculus over Iran
item.description: In the Middle East, it is a political weakling but an economic force

item.title: In Iran, Donald Trump is making history
item.description: Too bad he has so little appreciation for its lessons

item.title: War, succession and the perilous test of two myths about Iran
item.description: America wagers on collapse; the regime on endurance

item.title: Why Donald Trump gambled in Iran
item.description: The disruptor-in-chief wants to send a message to enemies everywhere by demonstrating the devastating nature of American power

item.title: Gavin Newsom wants to reintroduce himself
item.description: The governor has become the de facto leader of the Democratic Party

item.title: At last, reasons to be cheerful about European tech
item.description: One of which is Donald Trump

item.title: War in Iran could cause the biggest oil shock in years
item.description: Prices have surged—and may stay high for a while

item.title: Ali Khamenei may be dead, but Donald Trump has unfinished business
item.description: The president has declared bombing will continue until it brings “peace”

item.title: China hopes IVF can slow its baby bust
item.description: That may be overambitious

item.title: A cancer diagnosis can push people to crime
item.description: Even generous welfare states are not immune to a “Breaking Bad” effect

item.title: India, the world’s most colourful country, is changing its hues
item.description: Its transformation can be seen in its evolving palette

item.title: Ali Khamenei grabbed power and held it, at bloody cost
item.description: Iran’s supreme leader died on February 28th, aged 86

item.title: With the supreme leader dead, power in Iran hangs in the balance
item.description: A dramatic blow to the regime—followed by dangerous uncertainty

item.title: America’s Gulf allies face a moment of great peril
item.description: If war cannot be avoided, should they take sides?

item.title: America and Israel bomb Iran, aiming to topple its regime
item.description: Donald Trump calls on Iranians to rise up. War could quickly engulf the Middle East

item.title: Donald Trump lashes out at Anthropic
item.description: Silicon Valley will suffer as a consequence

item.title: What a Warner Bros-Paramount colossus would look like
item.description: Hollywood’s new giant will be indebted and unwieldy

item.title: What does “open war” between Pakistan and Afghanistan amount to?
item.description: The bombing of military sites in Kabul and Kandahar marks a sharp escalation in tensions

item.title: Will magnesium supplements help you relax?
item.description: Not everyone will feel the benefits

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Do ceasefires actually work?
item.description: Richard Cockett, a senior editor, considers whether one is possible in Ukraine

item.title: Brazil’s almighty Supreme Court must win back public trust
item.description: Recent scandals have tarnished the image of one of Brazil’s most important democratic institutions

item.title: What North Korea’s mysterious party congress reveals
item.description: Kim Jong Un uses a five-yearly gathering to boost his daughter, and more

item.title: America’s states should beware of copying Europe too much
item.description: Welfare is rightly becoming more generous. But regulatory fragmentation is a problem

item.title: Heathrow’s third runway is turning into another infrastructure fiasco
item.description: The government must step in

item.title: Google Maps makes another pitch for better South Korean data
item.description: Now Donald Trump is on Google’s side

item.title: China piles pressure on Japan after Takaichi Sanae’s triumph
item.description: The latest feud between the East Asian giants is unlikely to end soon

item.title: Mapping China’s holiday rush
item.description: How travel during the Spring Festival is changing

item.title: Who speaks for the Muslim world?
item.description: There are many contenders, but Turkey’s president leads the pack

item.title: The battle to flip Texas
item.description: Democrats have a chance to score a stunning upset in November’s midterms

item.title: The Trump court? Not quite
item.description: The tariffs ruling reveals justices split over doctrine, not Donald Trump

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
How the war in Ukraine affects Siberian Russia
item.description: It has changed Yakutia, but not its core

item.title: Ukraine is scaling up interceptor drones
item.description: Machines that kill are the next frontier

item.title: Giorgia Meloni is taking on the courts in Italy
item.description: Italy’s referendum will do nothing to speed up its sluggish judiciary

item.title: Tony Robbins, the megalosaurus of motivation
item.description: Watching the world’s best-known life coach do his thing

item.title: The stunning rise of China’s most audacious miner
item.description: Zijin Mining is giving Western giants a run for their money

item.title: The Sphere is taking its success in Las Vegas to the world
item.description: Who will want a hundred-metre-high orb?

item.title: Iran may insist Hizbullah fights on its behalf
item.description: But the cost at home for Iran’s proxy in Lebanon will be high

item.title: South Sudan’s decrepit regime is unravelling
item.description: It is taking the country down with it

item.title: Iranians’ angry defiance is growing once again
item.description: Before missiles were launched, the regime was making few concessions

item.title: Ali Larijani is an increasingly plausible heir in Iran
item.description: But hardliners have never fully trusted him

item.title: Donald Trump’s oil embargo reveals a solar boom in Cuba
item.description: Chinese solar panels are flooding into the troubled island nation

item.title: Each year tens of thousands of Americans accidentally kill
item.description: The psychological toll it takes is huge—and mostly hidden

item.title: Donald Trump is at risk of launching a war without purpose
item.description: A conflict with Iran without a clear objective would be recklessly dangerous

item.title: The fake-meat industry is in trouble
item.description: Business is slowing down. Time to tempt new palates

item.title: SOS for India’s Pink City
item.description: A threat from UNESCO might prod Jaipur into saving its heritage

item.title: America’s trade chaos is just beginning
item.description: Tariff wrangling will stretch through the rest of Donald Trump’s term, and beyond

item.title: Protectionists dislike trade and migration. And capital flows?
item.description: The amount of money crossing borders has flattened off—but not because of capital controls

item.title: Why Chinese people spend so much on food
item.description: A 21st-century test of a 19th-century observation

item.title: America’s dangerous pursuit of critical-mineral dominance
item.description: With a more focused approach, it could break China’s chokehold

item.title: America’s new era of state-sponsored mining
item.description: Desperate to break China’s grip on critical minerals, the Trump administration is splashing cash around the world

item.title: The right response to private-market dangers
item.description: Was a Blue Owl fund mismanaged, or did it reveal fundamental problems about the industry?

item.title: America’s welfare state is more European than you think
item.description: State-level policies are making up for stingy federal provision

item.title: A viral research note on AI gets its economics wrong
item.description: Too much of a good thing

item.title: Luxury goods are Europe’s global tax on vanity
item.description: How to trade cachet for dollars and yuan

item.title: American labs say China’s AI tigers are copycats
item.description: DeepSeek’s new model has American officials and firms on edge

item.title: America’s bosses are being dragged into local politics
item.description: Arise, CEO-councilmen

item.title: Americans have no idea what Donald Trump wants from Iran
item.description: The president himself may be among them

item.title: Marks left by Stone Age humans were surprisingly complex
item.description: Their information density rivals the immediate predecessors of writing

item.title: Will one-stop blood tests for cancer save lives?
item.description: They are increasingly popular

item.title: AI tools are being prepared for the physical world
item.description: The race to build world models is on

item.title: Our language analysis of Donald Trump’s state-of-the-union address
item.description: His record-breaking speech was light on policy and heavy on theatre

item.title: Modernisation is making South-East Asia more Islamic
item.description: Economic growth in Malaysia and Indonesia has not led to secularism

item.title: Donald Trump’s unworthy state of the union
item.description: An address not fit for America’s 250th birthday

item.title: A stay-calm plan to save the world
item.description: Alas, almost no foreign leader is as reasonable as Finland’s president, Alexander Stubb

item.title: Brazil’s high court is caught up in a vast scandal
item.description: Some of the world’s most powerful judges have an over-cosy relationship with business and political elites

item.title: It’s California’s 250th birthday, too
item.description: Kind of

item.title: Pete Hegseth wages war on Anthropic
item.description: Should AI labs unquestioningly obey the Pentagon’s orders?

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: An interview with Zambia’s president
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, talks to Hakainde Hichilema

item.title: How to get rich in modern China
item.description: Some of the country’s brightest are cashing in on a state-backed surge

item.title: Bosses should not hold their breath for a Trump tariff refund
item.description: The process will be slow and politically fraught

item.title: The war against PDFs is heating up
item.description: Will the file type survive the AI revolution?

item.title: How Russia’s fatalities compare with Ukraine’s
item.description: After four years of fighting, our modelling reveals a grim toll

item.title: The War Room newsletter: What is Donald Trump’s aim for Iran?
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, analyses the president’s options

item.title: Where the DHS shutdown could start to hurt
item.description: So far the budget impasse has caused little public outcry. That may soon change

item.title: France’s far left reckons with the murder of a far-right activist
item.description: Quentin Deranque’s death forces the left to confront its extremists

item.title: The rotten tail of China’s property bust
item.description: Officials want to spread the pain as widely as possible

item.title: The killing of Mexico’s most powerful narco will please Donald Trump
item.description: But it risks provoking terrible violence in Mexico

item.title: Rejoice! Private equity is taking over America’s small businesses
item.description: Its ghoulish reputation is ill-deserved

item.title: What are Donald Trump’s strike options in Iran?
item.description: An immense armada threatens the regime, but it is not yet blinking

item.title: Why one corner of Europe’s car industry is still booming
item.description: The world’s most car-intensive economy draws plenty of investment, for now

item.title: The AI productivity boom is not here (yet)
item.description: Artificial intelligence is improving fast. Its effect on output, not so much

item.title: Markets are churning furiously beneath the surface
item.description: AI is prompting investors to reassess every business model under the sun

item.title: India’s VIP culture is out of control
item.description: The country’s leaders claim to be servants but act like masters

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Putin’s forever war
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s covers

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Jesse Jackson and the great racial backlash
item.description: Daniel Knowles, our Midwest correspondent, on why the civil-rights fight feels newly urgent

item.title: The Midwest’s remarkable turnaround
item.description: For the first time in 70 years, more Americans are coming than going

item.title: Donald Trump answers a Supreme Court rebuke with new tariff threats
item.description: The immediate economic impact will be more uncertainty

item.title: Should you be fibremaxxing?
item.description: What to make of the new dietary fad

item.title: The moment of reckoning between America and Iran
item.description: Donald Trump must soon choose whether to attack Iran or beat a humiliating retreat

item.title: A psychedelic medicine performs well against depression
item.description: Regulators are now mulling the results of the late-stage trial

item.title: How to improve American legislators’ lot
item.description: Doing so would be good for members of Congress, and for democracy

item.title: Saudi Arabia and the Emirates must resolve their own differences
item.description: America’s neglect is allowing an unwelcome tension to fester between two of its allies

item.title: Could One Nation soon become Australia’s most popular party?
item.description: The anti-immigrant party is dragging the Liberals rightward as well

item.title: A nasty spate of shark attacks in the Sydney area
item.description: Is global warming to blame?

item.title: Can Bangladesh’s old guard build a new democracy?
item.description: The first real election since 2008 is won by a prodigal son

item.title: China now fills the world’s luxury hampers
item.description: It is a leading producer of caviar, foie gras and truffles

item.title: Different ideas about faith are dividing Republicans over Israel
item.description: The Republicans’ internal fight could prove more toxic than the Democrats’

item.title: The Trump administration wants to put antifa on trial
item.description: A case in Texas offers an opportunity to do so

item.title: Donald Trump is reshaping American health care
item.description: And the consequences will ripple through both health and politics

Politics
Business
Serbia’s protesters learn it’s hard to topple a president
item.description: President Aleksandar Vucic is trying to outlast fury over corruption

item.title: How Germany fell out of love with China
item.description: Chinese industry is beating Germany at its own game. Cue panic

item.title: Poles have split and soured on America
item.description: Donald Trump is exhausting goodwill in Europe’s most pro-American country

item.title: Could the next big gambling destination be in the Gulf?
item.description: The UAE hopes to emulate Las Vegas and Macau

item.title: Giorgio Armani’s bizarre will has caused a rift at his fashion label
item.description: The choices its new leaders make could shape the future of Italian luxury

item.description: Stop optimising. Start pronking

item.title: The global triumph of Nigerian fashion
item.description: Its flamboyant styles are gracing red carpets and going viral on TikTok

item.title: A book fair in Damascus is a window on the new Syria
item.description: But many worry it will not stay open for long

item.title: Libya has no good options for leaders
item.description: Mourning at the death of a man indicted for crimes against humanity highlights Libya’s bleakness

item.title: Peru ousts a president under the shadow of Chinese meddling
item.description: José Jerí was the country’s eighth president in a decade.

item.title: How four years of war have changed Russia
item.description: The fighting in Ukraine has reshaped everyday life

item.title: India is in the midst of a data-centre investment boom
item.description: Money is flowing into the country, but the benefits may prove temporary

item.title: The EU is thrashing out a more muscular set of economic policies
item.description: The bloc is done playing nicely

item.title: Did America’s war on poverty fail?
item.description: Deprivation has fallen dramatically—but not necessarily because of the welfare state

item.title: Why the IMF’s newest report finds that the yuan is undervalued
item.description: It says the currency is 16% cheaper than it should be

item.title: Prediction markets are rife with insider betting
item.description: That does not mean regulators should stamp it out

item.title: Vladimir Putin is caught in a vice of his own making
item.description: Russia’s president cannot win the war, but fears peace

item.title: Don’t go after the rich to fix broken budgets
item.description: It will not work, and is wrong in principle

item.title: Welcome to the era of anarchic antitrust
item.description: Competition enforcement in America has descended into chaos

item.title: Why insider trading isn’t always bad
item.description: At least on prediction markets

item.title: That irritating feeling that France was right
item.description: Donald Trump’s America makes Gaullism respectable again

item.title: South Korea is still haunted by its disgraced ex-president
item.description: A guilty verdict will not settle Yoon Suk Yeol’s toxic legacy

item.title: China’s humanoids are dazzling the world. Who will buy them?
item.description: The market for robot dancers, alas, is limited

item.title: Brain-like computers could be built out of perovskites
item.description: The long-hyped materials may have found their niche

item.title: The Human Exposome Project will map how environmental factors shape health
item.description: It makes the Human Genome Project look easy

item.title: How ICE’s new software tools could speed up deportations
item.description: The risk of overreach is high

item.title: The front line in America’s child-vaccine battle
item.description: How far can RFK junior push state policies? West Virginia will provide an answer

item.title: How a four-year onslaught has changed Ukraine
item.description: The border guards who faced Russia’s tanks were changed for ever, like their country

item.title: Russia’s economy has entered the death zone
item.description: Alexandra Prokopenko wonders how much longer it can go on metabolising its own muscle tissue

item.title: Donald Trump’s envoys failed to reassure Europe
item.description: A gathering of world leaders in Munich shows how trust has collapsed

item.title: Why the Gulf’s most powerful countries are at odds
item.description: A feud between Saudi Arabia and the UAE will have far-reaching consequences

item.title: How big is the prize of reopening Russia?
item.description: The Kremlin is promising $12trn-worth of deals to Donald Trump’s administration

item.title: The financialisation of AI is just beginning
item.description: Get ready for a new wave of securities, hedges and collateral

item.title: Beware China’s shrinking car market
item.description: The resulting flood of exports will be good news for consumers. Less so for the world’s carmakers

item.title: Off the Charts newsletter: Coping with outliers
item.description: Sarah Leo, our visual data journalist, explains how to deal with unruly data points

item.title: The flaws in India’s AI plans
item.description: Despite hosting a global summit, it is a bystander in the race to develop frontier models

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Is a peace deal possible?
item.description: Oliver Carroll, our foreign correspondent, analyses the prospects—and pitfalls—of ending the war in Ukraine

item.title: The Robin Hood state is coming for the rich
item.description: Tax systems are more progressive than you think

item.title: The crummiest job in Washington—congressman—is getting worse
item.description: Longer hours, lower pay, more threats and less power

item.title: Americans are unleashing their anger on food-delivery robots
item.description: The machines don’t deserve it

item.title: Nicaragua has so far dodged the fate of Cuba and Venezuela
item.description: Latin America’s other leftist autocracy has not yet come under sustained pressure from the United States

item.title: Why American allies are flocking to see Xi Jinping in Beijing
item.description: China isn’t using its considerable leverage over them—yet

item.title: Dubai’s crazy rich Chinese
item.description: Scrutinised at home and in the West, Chinese money is moving into the Gulf

item.title: Donald Trump’s schemes to juice the economy
item.description: Watch out for sneaky stimulus

item.title: Checks and Balance: The death of the “endangerment finding”
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, says the rollback of climate regulation reveals a deeper story about American governance

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The most powerful woman in the world
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s covers

item.title: Why MAGA brands have been a flop
item.description: Conservatives are better at trashing businesses than building them

item.title: Homegrown apps are making dating in India less awkward
item.description: AI tools compose love poems on behalf of tongue-tied suitors

item.title: Who really won the war between India and Pakistan?
item.description: The dispute over that dispute bodes ill for the next fight

item.title: An audacious new book about a “precocious” country
item.description: There is no such thing as “the” Indian growth model

item.title: National job stereotypes need updating
item.description: Unemployment rates in rich countries are becoming topsy-turvy

item.title: The ascent of India’s economy
item.description: It has benefited from a slice of luck, a commitment to economic reform and a shove from Donald Trump

item.title: Donald Trump’s expansionist itch has undermined global security
item.description: He has suspended his predation of Greenland, but he cannot retract the mistrust it generated

item.title: Trump’s Board of Peace is a distraction from the real work in Gaza
item.description: America’s president has unusual power to impose peace; he must continue to use it

item.title: Lisa Cook’s job at the Federal Reserve looks safe
item.description: The justices sound sceptical about Donald Trump’s move to dismiss her

item.title: The true danger posed by Donald Trump
item.description: Despite a tactical retreat, great risks remain

item.title: Donald Trump’s grab for Greenland makes no sense
item.description: It would harm America’s security and its economy

item.title: TikTok is still a danger. America no longer cares
item.description: The app will be owned by allies of the president but retain ties to China

item.title: American decay versus American dynamism
item.description: Only a decline in corporate America’s vigour will dent the country’s dominance of financial markets

item.title: Why Minneapolis is at the centre of Donald Trump’s immigration crackdown
item.description: The administration is tormenting, and testing, a unique political culture

item.title: Satellites encased in wood are in the works
item.description: Timber is cheaper and better than alloys, and may be less polluting

item.title: To disperse their spores, truffles rely on animals eating other animals
item.description: It is a gruesome business

item.title: A new study highlights the brain’s role in immune health
item.description: It truly is a question of mind over matter

item.title: The remarkable recovery of Narendra Modi
item.description: Despite an electoral reverse in 2024, India’s prime minister seems dominant

item.title: Japan’s bond-market tremble reflects a fiscal-monetary clash
item.description: As investors worry about budgetary laxity, the central bank prepares for more rate hikes

item.title: Western leaders navigate a lonely world
item.description: China cannot and will not save mid-size American allies from Donald Trump

item.title: Canadian soldiers are subject to Donald Trump’s orders
item.description: As the American president becomes more aggressive, this is becoming a problem

item.title: Why the beauty industry is booming
item.description: Plain bottles with clinical labels are part of the secret

item.title: Donald Trump’s bullying is irritating his European populist chums
item.description: French and German nationalists are not happy with America’s grab for Greenland

item.title: Denmark braces for Donald Trump’s Greenland tariffs
item.description: The economy is well prepared, but more exposed than it used to be
